title: Numbers in Arabic Grammar: Masculine-Feminine Opposites and Practical Application
summary: The session focused on applying masculine-feminsin number rules to female nouns. Students practiced forming numbers from three to ten with feminine ma'dood, emphasizing the opposite gender rule for numbers. The teacher used examples like 'four students' (arba'atu talibat) and discussed pluralization in specific contexts, clarifying common mistakes regarding tanween and mudaf-mudaf ilayh rules.
key_points: Numbers from three to ten follow a masculine-feminine opposite rule when counting nouns. For female ma'dood, use male numbers (e.g., arba'atu talibat).
Pluralization of counted nouns must agree with the singular gender: if the singular is feminine, the plural number takes kasrah (e.g., sab'atu riyalatin).
Key terms like mudaf (mafuu') and mudaf ilayh were reviewed in examples such as khamsatun a'man.
Prayer-related vocabulary (e.g., rajad, rubbana) was discussed in context of religious text applications.
questions: [00:03:00] Q: How many brothers do you have? — A: Use female numbers for male plural nouns (e.g., khamsatu ikhwatin).
[00:19:00] Q: Why is 'arba'atu a'mamin' correct instead of 'arba'un'? — A: Plural feminine requires male number with kasrah.

quiz.q: What is the key rule for numbers from 3 to 10 when counting nouns (maʿdūd) in the lesson?
options: The number matches the gender of the noun
The number is always masculine regardless of noun
The number is the opposite gender of the noun
quiz.a: The teacher explains that numbers 3–10 follow the 'opposite gender' rule: masculine number with feminine noun and vice versa.

quiz.q: When the maʿdūd is feminine, what form should the number take?
quiz.a: The lesson repeatedly shows that when the noun is feminine, the number becomes masculine (e.g., thalāthatu ṭālibāt).

quiz.q: What does the term 'baina al-qawsayn' mean in the instructions?
quiz.a: The teacher explicitly explains 'baina al-qawsayn' means 'between the brackets' where numbers are given.

quiz.q: What is the plural of سُؤَال (suʾāl) used frequently in the lesson?
quiz.a: The teacher highlights that أَسْئِلَة (asʾila) is the plural form of suʾāl and appears in headings like 'ajib ʿan al-asʾila'.

quiz.q: What rule applies to a noun in a mudāf–mudāf ilayh construction regarding tanwīn?
quiz.a: The teacher states that when a word is mudāf, it cannot take tanwīn (e.g., abnāʾin becomes abnāʾ).

quiz.q: What is the meaning of the adverb قَبْلَ (qabla) as used in the lesson?
quiz.a: The teacher explains qabla means 'before' and is used for time expressions like 'a month ago'.

quiz.q: What determines the verb form when context clues are missing in Arabic sentences?
options: Context such as who is being addressed
quiz.a: The teacher emphasizes using context clues like gender and addressee to determine correct verb forms.

quiz.q: In the lesson, what does رُكْبَانًا (rukbānan) refer to?
quiz.a: The teacher explains rukbānan as riders, used in the Qur’ānic context of praying while riding in fear situations.

quiz.q: What is the main idea behind using opposite-gender agreement in Arabic numbers (3–10)?
options: To make Arabic easier for English speakers
It is a fixed grammatical rule for number agreement
It applies only in Qur’ānic Arabic
quiz.a: The lesson stresses this as a core grammatical rule: numbers 3–10 must oppose the gender of the counted noun.

quran_examples.arabic: وَلَبِثُوا فِي كَهْفِهِمْ ثَلَاثَ مِائَةٍ سِنِينَ وَازْدَادُوا تِسْعًا
quran_examples.transliteration: wa labithū fī kahfihim thalātha miʾatin sinīna wa izdādū tisʿā
quran_examples.translation: And they remained in their cave for three hundred years and added nine.
quran_examples.note: Demonstrates numerical usage (thalātha, tisʿā) showing Arabic number agreement in context of counting.

quran_examples.arabic: فَإِنْ خِفْتُمْ فَرِجَالًا أَوْ رُكْبَانًا ۖ فَإِذَا أَمِنتُمْ فَاذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ كَمَا عَلَّمَكُمْ مَا لَمْ تَكُونُوا تَعْلَمُونَ
quran_examples.transliteration: fa in khiftum fa-rijālan aw rukbānan fa idhā amintum fa-udhkurū Allāha kamā ʿallamakum mā lam takūnū taʿlamūn
quran_examples.translation: And if you fear [an enemy], then pray on foot or riding. But when you are safe, remember Allah as He taught you what you did not know.
quran_examples.note: Directly matches the lesson’s discussion of praying while walking or riding (rukbān) under fear conditions.

quran_examples.arabic: يَسْأَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَالْمَيْسِرِ ۖ قُلْ فِيهِمَا إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَمَنَافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ
quran_examples.transliteration: yasʾalūnaka ʿani al-khamri wa al-maysir qul fīhimā ithmun kabīrun wa manāfiʿu lil-nās
quran_examples.translation: They ask you about wine and gambling. Say: In them is great sin and benefit for people.
quran_examples.note: Illustrates the plural form 'yasʾalūnaka' and connects to the lesson’s focus on asʾila (questions).
